pr.title: docs: operational capability readiness assessment for Merge, Deploy, Rollback, Payments, Entitlements, and Schema/DNS/Customer mutations
pr.body:
Adds `docs/operational-capability-readiness.md` — a codebase-evidence-grounded gap analysis assessing how close Rune is to executing six sensitive operational capabilities.

## Findings at a glance

| Capability | Rating | Blocker |
|---|---|---|
| Merge | 🟢 Ready | `GITHUB_TOKEN` + repo allowlist confirmation |
| Deploy | 🟡 Partial | Needs `RUNE_DEPLOYMENT_MUTATION_MODE=cli_runner` + external runner process |
| Rollback | 🟡 Partial | Same as Deploy |
| Change payments | 🔴 Not connected | No RevenueCat write client; read-only only |
| Grant entitlements | 🔴 Not connected | Same; no admin API key or write endpoints |
| Mutate schema/DNS/customer systems | 🔴 Not connected | Gate policies exist; no executor for any of the three |

## What the document contains

- **Per-capability sections**: current state, evidence citations (file + line), gaps/blockers, readiness rating, and ordered next steps
- **Capability matrix**: one-row-per-capability table covering gate policy, audit log, executor, runner dependency, and real integration status
- **Progress checklist**: pre-populated with what already exists (gate policies, audit wiring, dry-run enforcement, allowlist enforcement, CLI command allowlist) so contributors can track remaining items to completion

## Key cross-cutting observations captured

The gate and audit infrastructure (`lib/privileged-operations.ts`, `rune_action_events` table, `auditPrivilegedOperationGate`) is solid across all eight operation kinds. The split is clean: Merge and Deploy/Rollback have real executors; the payments/entitlements/mutation capabilities have only gate + audit stubs, consistent with what `RUNE_NOT_CONNECTED_YET` in `lib/project-registry.ts` already documents.

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> Analyze the repository `Tanjiro-1122/Rune` and create a pull request that adds a clear, actionable assessment of how far the project is from being able to support the following operational capabilities:
> 
> - Merge
> - Deploy
> - Rollback
> - Change payments
> - Grant entitlements
> - Mutate schemas/DNS/customer systems
> 
> The pull request should:
> 
> 1. Inspect the existing codebase, documentation, workflows, configuration, and any relevant artifacts in the repository.
> 2. Determine what infrastructure, code paths, interfaces, automation, permissions model, safety controls, auditability, and operator workflows already exist that are relevant to each capability listed above.
> 3. Identify missing pieces, blockers, risks, and assumptions for each capability.
> 4. Produce a concise but useful artifact in the repository (for example, a markdown assessment document) summarizing:
>    - current state
>    - evidence found in the repo
>    - gaps
>    - estimated readiness / distance to capability
>    - recommended next steps
> 5. If appropriate, add lightweight structure such as a capability matrix or checklist so future contributors can track progress.
> 6. Avoid making speculative claims that are not grounded in repository evidence; clearly distinguish evidence from inference.
> 7. Keep the change scoped to analysis/documentation unless the repo already has an obvious place for this assessment.
> 
> Create the pull request against the repository with a concise title reflecting this readiness assessment work.
> 
> The following is the prior conversation context from the user's chat exploration (may be truncated):
> 
> User: Create pr to analyze rune and see how far is it from being able to Merge
> Deploy
> Rollback
> Change payments
> Grant entitlements
> Mutate schemas/DNS/customer systems
> 
> Assistant: I’ve started the PR creation flow and am waiting for your response to the confirmation dialog before I can continue.
> 
> 


</details>



<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT SUFFIX -->

*This pull request was created from Copilot chat.*
>
